Phyt..personality: A deeply physically reactive constitution where emotional and environmental stresses manifest immediately in the glandular system, particularly the breasts. The Phytolacca patient is restless and uncomfortable, caught between sensitivity to both heat and cold, never finding a comfortable state—analogous to the mercurial temperament but rooted in a slower, more indurative process.
Phyt..mental_state: Kent explicitly notes that the mental symptoms have not been fully brought out in provings, leaving this area fragmentary. What emerges indirectly is a state of dull, heavy suffering with bone-deep weariness, worsening at night and from warmth of the bed, producing a restless inability to find relief. The patient may feel dizzy and sick when trying to sit up, suggesting a mental fog and prostration under acute illness.
Phyt..emotional_pattern: Every emotional shock—fear, fright, accident, or tribulation—translates directly into physical glandular inflammation rather than being processed psychologically. This somatization pattern suggests a constitution that suppresses or bypasses emotional expression, channeling distress into the body. There is an underlying vulnerability and hypersensitivity to upset, where even modest excitement produces disproportionate physical consequences.
